items.name: Byline Cutback
items.blurb: Reach the byline and pull the ball back low to the penalty spot against defenders facing their own goal.
items.when_to_use: Best against a set low block, since defenders must turn to face their own goal to defend it.
coaching_points_json: Statistically the highest value delivery in football: send one runner to the spot and one late to the edge.
Cross into the corridor, not to a man, so the runner's job is to meet it.
items.youth_takeaway: The simplest cross is often the best one: pull it back low and let the runner arrive.

steps.caption: The crosser reaches the byline before delivering.
The ball is pulled back low to the penalty spot for the arriving runner.

items.name: Whipped Early Cross
items.blurb: Hit early from deep or wide with pace, curled into the corridor between the keeper and a retreating back line.
items.when_to_use: Against a defence retreating at speed, since a defender facing his own goal cannot set his feet.
coaching_points_json: No touch should be needed: the runner's movement alone should meet the ball.
A half beat early beats a perfect ball delivered late.
items.youth_takeaway: Speed of delivery matters as much as accuracy: beat the defence back before it can reorganise.

steps.caption: The ball is struck before the defensive block can set.
Curled with pace into the corridor between the keeper and the retreating line.

items.name: Low Driven Cross / Flash Ball
items.blurb: A flat, hard ball is driven across the six-yard box so nobody needs to win a header, only make contact.
items.when_to_use: Fits pressing and vertical identities, since even a missed touch becomes a live second ball.
coaching_points_json: Weight the ball so any contact redirects it goal-ward.
Runners arrive at pace and never stand waiting for it.
items.youth_takeaway: A hard, flat cross turns a difficult header into an easy touch.

steps.caption: The ball is struck flat and hard across the six-yard box.

items.name: Floated Back-Post Cross
items.blurb: The ball is hung up high toward the far post for an aerial mismatch or a ghosting late arrival.
items.when_to_use: When there is an aerial mismatch to isolate away from the goalkeeper, or a ghosting run to exploit.
coaching_points_json: Aim for the far-post corridor, beyond the far centre back's vision.
Pairs naturally with a target man and a big-and-little strike partnership.
items.youth_takeaway: Not every cross should go to the near post: the far post is often unguarded.

steps.caption: The ball is floated up and toward the far post.

items.name: In-swinger vs Out-swinger
items.blurb: An inverted player curls the cross in toward goal, while a natural-side player curls it away and safer for headers.
items.when_to_use: Read from the crosser's preferred foot and side, a coaching detail already sitting in the roster data.
coaching_points_json: An in-swinger is attackable in stride but risks the keeper claiming it.
An out-swinger curls away from goal, safer for the keeper but ideal for arriving headers.
items.youth_takeaway: Which foot a player favours changes what kind of cross they should hit from each side.

steps.caption: An inverted crosser curls the ball in toward goal, dangerous but attackable in stride.

extras_json.footedness_rule: An inverted crosser (opposite foot to side) delivers in-swinging; a natural-side crosser delivers out-swinging. Card copy only, no automatic logic in this build.

items.name: Deep Diagonal Cross
items.blurb: A switch of play is aimed directly into the box rather than to feet, for a far-post winger or wingback.
items.when_to_use: The home of back-three identities, where a far-side wingback attacks the ball directly.
coaching_points_json: This is a switch of play aimed at the box, not at feet.
The far-side runner must already be moving to attack the ball in the air.
items.youth_takeaway: A switch does not have to land at feet: sometimes the best target is the box itself.

steps.caption: A long diagonal switch is aimed directly into the box rather than to feet.

items.name: Slide-Rule Through Ball
items.blurb: A flat ball is threaded into the channel between fullback and centre back, weighted to the runner's stride.
items.when_to_use: The bread and butter of the feeder and finisher pairing, needing real passing range from the passer.
coaching_points_json: Weight the ball to the runner's stride, not to a fixed spot.
The runner times his run off the passer's body shape, not the ball's release.
items.youth_takeaway: A perfectly weighted pass into space is worth more than a perfectly weighted pass to feet.

steps.caption: A flat pass is threaded into the channel between fullback and centre back.

items.name: Ball Over the Top
items.blurb: An aerial ball is clipped over a high defensive line into space, the counter identity's forty-yard dagger.
items.when_to_use: The punishment for a bad press, and the counter identity's signature long-range weapon.
coaching_points_json: The pass must be airborne before the defensive shape has turned to face it.
The runner's timing beats the defender's recovery pace, not the other way around.
items.youth_takeaway: A ball played early into space beats a ball played late to a marked player.

steps.caption: The ball is clipped high over the defensive line before the back line's shape can turn.
